title: The Right Remedy
description:
The engineering team submitted four proposed fixes for the SQL injection vulnerability in the login form. Amaka returns three of them with a red mark. One — and only one — actually resolves the root cause.

The vulnerability: the application builds its SQL query by concatenating user-supplied strings directly into the query text. The fix must address why the injection was possible in the first place, not just what characters the attacker used.

choices: Replace string concatenation with parameterised queries (prepared statements) throughout the data access layer — the database driver then treats user input as a value, never as executable query syntax, regardless of what characters it contains.

Add a server-side input validator that rejects any login attempt containing apostrophes, double dashes, or the OR keyword — these characters and keywords are what the attacker used to manipulate the query.
Rate-limit the login endpoint to three attempts per IP per minute and add CAPTCHA — this prevents the attacker from sending enough requests to exploit the weakness before being blocked.
Switch to an ORM that auto-generates SQL — ORMs produce safe queries by design, so the underlying string concatenation code does not need to be changed.
